text:
The docking clamps engage with a sound you almost remember. The station looks the same from outside — same silhouette against the starfield, same running lights cycling through their pattern. But the frequency is different. Someone changed the timing.

Inside, the corridors have been repainted. The old murals are gone, covered by something cleaner and less interesting. A directory board lists businesses you don't recognize. The air recyclers have a different smell — not unpleasant, just not the one you knew.

A maintenance worker glances at your ship registry, then at you, then back at the registry. She doesn't say anything. She just nods, slowly, and goes back to her work.

You're listed in the station's historical records, probably. A name from before she was born.

choices.label: Ask about the old businesses
choices.tone_note: Nostalgia. The player is reaching for something that isn't there.

effects.description: Spoke to locals about the station's history. Names and dates that connect to a time before you arrived here last.

effects.text: Learned what changed. It was everything.

choices.label: Head to the trade board
choices.tone_note: Pragmatic. Moving forward.

effects.text: Resupplied. The ship feels a little more whole.

choices.label: Find the historical records
choices.tone_note: Looking for evidence you were here. That it mattered.

effects.description: Found your name in the station's historical records. You were here. That much is documented.

effects.text: The records confirmed what you already knew. You passed through here once, when it meant something different.

choices.label: Return to the ship
choices.tone_note: Some places you can't go back to.

text:
Your comms officer adjusts her console twice before saying anything.

"Signal. Automated distress beacon, standard Hegemony format. But the encoding is... old. This protocol was deprecated decades ago."

The signal resolves into coordinates — a point in the outer system, near the gas giant's third moon. No ship appears on sensors. Just the signal, repeating patiently in a language nobody uses anymore.

It could be a ship that's been drifting since before you left your last port. It could be a lure. It could be someone who's been waiting a very long time for help that was never coming.

choices.label: Investigate the coordinates
choices.tone_note: Committing. Whatever it is, you'll know.

effects.description: Investigated the signal source. Found something — wreckage, or a beacon, or something stranger. The data needs analysis.

effects.text: Followed the signal. What you found raises more questions than it answers.

choices.label: Scan from here first
choices.tone_note: Cautious. Information before commitment.

effects.description: Long-range scan of the signal source reveals metallic mass, no power signature, and an age estimate that doesn't match anything in the records.

effects.text: Scanned from a safe distance. The data is strange but useful.

choices.label: Log it and move on
choices.tone_note: You can't save everyone. You learned that a long time ago.

effects.text: Noted the coordinates. Moved on.

text:
A freighter captain catches you at the docking bay airlock. She's got the look of someone who's been waiting for any ship to pull in — tired, caffeinated, leaning against a cargo loader like it's the only thing keeping her upright.

"You heading rimward? I've got medical supplies that were supposed to go to Thornfield three months ago. Contractor fell through. I can't make the run myself — engine coil's shot. Pay's decent. Nothing fancy, just getting boxes from here to there."

She slides a manifest across. Standard pharmaceuticals, sealed crates. The kind of cargo that's straightforward until it isn't.

choices.label: Accept the job
choices.tone_note: Honest work. Straightforward. Probably.

effects.description: Accepted a cargo run to Thornfield — medical supplies, straightforward delivery. Someone is waiting.

effects.text: Loaded the cargo. Another obligation, another reason to keep moving.

choices.label: Ask why the contractor fell through
choices.tone_note: Trust but verify.

effects.description: Asked questions about the cargo job. The contractor fell through because the route passes near restricted space. The captain didn't mention that part.

effects.text: Dug a little deeper. Found a reason to be cautious.

choices.label: Decline politely
choices.tone_note: Not your problem.

effects.text: You declined. Life goes on.

text:
Nothing.

A red dwarf at {system.name} and its handful of frozen worlds. No signals. No stations. No evidence that anything with a pulse has ever been here. Your sensors sweep the system and come back with data that amounts to a shrug.

Your navigator marks the system as surveyed and adds it to the charts. Another dot on a map that's mostly empty space. The star looks old and tired and very far from anything that matters.

The ship hums around you. The crew goes about their routines. Outside the viewport, the galaxy continues not to notice you.

choices.label: Run a detailed survey anyway
choices.tone_note: Thoroughness for its own sake. Or maybe hoping to find something.

effects.description: The survey picked up something faint — a signal signature that doesn't match any known source.

effects.text: Thorough scan completed. Found more than expected.

choices.label: Sit with the quiet for a while
choices.tone_note: Sometimes the emptiness is the point.

effects.text: Took a moment. The universe waited.

choices.label: Plot the next jump
choices.tone_note: Keep moving. Standing still in the void gets uncomfortable.

text:
The fuel depot is operational but the prices on the board make your quartermaster wince audibly.

"Supply chain issues," the depot manager says, not looking up from his console. "The Hegemony rerouted the tanker convoys six months ago. Something about security concerns. So now we get fuel when we get fuel, and we charge what the market will bear."

He looks at your ship's registration. Looks at your fuel gauge readout on his screen.

"Looks like the market will bear quite a lot, in your case."

choices.label: Pay the inflated price
choices.tone_note: You don't have a choice and you both know it.

effects.text: Paid through the nose, but the tanks are fuller.

choices.label: Negotiate
choices.tone_note: Worth trying. Dignity costs nothing.

effects.text: Talked the price down. Not cheap, but fair enough.

choices.label: Ask about alternative fuel sources
choices.tone_note: There's always another way. Usually a worse one.

effects.description: Heard about an alternative fuel source — unorthodox, possibly dangerous, worth investigating.

effects.text: No fuel today, but a lead on something interesting.

choices.label: Buy only what you need to reach the next system
choices.tone_note: Calculated risk. Hope the next port is cheaper.

effects.text: Just enough to reach the next port. Hopefully.

text:
It doesn't show up on sensors until you're almost on top of it.

A structure, orbiting the outermost planet at a distance that shouldn't be stable. No power readings. No heat signature. But it's there — angular, enormous, made of something your sensors can't identify. The material doesn't reflect light the way anything should. It's like looking at a hole in the shape of architecture.

Your science officer pulls up comparative databases and comes back with nothing. No known civilization has built with this material. No record of this structure in any survey.

It's been here longer than anyone has been looking.

The crew is quiet. Everyone finds a reason to be near a viewport.

choices.label: Approach and attempt to dock
choices.tone_note: Awe overriding caution. This is why you're out here.

effects.description: Docked with an ancient structure of unknown origin. The interior defies easy description. Sensors recorded data that will take weeks to analyze.

effects.text: You went inside. What you found will take time to understand.

choices.label: Send a probe first
choices.tone_note: Fascination with restraint.

effects.description: Probe returned data from the ancient structure. Partial readings — enough to confirm it's artificial, not enough to explain it.

effects.text: The probe came back. Most of the data is incomprehensible.

choices.label: Record everything and keep your distance
choices.tone_note: Some things you document. Some things you don't touch.

effects.description: Recorded external observations of an ancient structure. Detailed but distant.

effects.text: Documented everything from a safe distance.

choices.label: Leave immediately
choices.tone_note: Fear. The smart kind.

effects.text: Burned hard and got clear. Cost fuel, saved everything else.

text:
Two {faction.name} patrol ships slide into formation around you before your comms officer can even open a channel.

"Vessel, you are entering controlled space. Transmit registration, cargo manifest, and crew roster. Do not deviate from your current heading."

The voice is professional but flat — someone who says this sentence forty times a shift. Your ship's registration is valid, probably. Your cargo is legal, mostly. Your crew roster is accurate, as far as anyone knows.

The patrol ships match your speed exactly. Patient. Unhurried. They have nowhere else to be.

choices.label: Comply fully — transmit everything
choices.tone_note: Nothing to hide. Or at least nothing they'll find.

effects.text: Inspection passed without incident.

choices.label: Comply but ask questions
choices.tone_note: Cooperative but not submissive.

effects.description: The checkpoint officer mentioned something interesting — increased patrols, a missing ship, a restricted zone that wasn't restricted last year.

effects.text: Complied and asked questions. Learned something worth knowing.

choices.label: Transmit registration only — cite independent vessel rights
choices.tone_note: Principled. Possibly stupid.

effects.description: Cited independent vessel rights at a faction checkpoint. They let you through, but they remembered your ship.

effects.text: Stood on principle. Made an impression. Not sure what kind.

text:
The ship is dead. No power, no atmosphere, turning slowly end over end in a decaying orbit. Your sensors read the hull markings — a freighter, civilian registration, last port of call unknown.

The damage isn't weapons fire. It's neglect. Systems failed one by one over what looks like months. The logs, if any survived, might tell the story. Or they might just be a list of things that stopped working, ending mid-sentence.

Someone lived here once. Flew this ship, ate meals in that galley, slept in those quarters. Chose a heading and opened the throttle and believed they were going somewhere that mattered.

Your engineer says it might have salvageable parts. Your navigator says nothing.

choices.label: Board and search for survivors or logs
choices.tone_note: Obligation. Or curiosity wearing the mask of obligation.

effects.description: Found something on the derelict — a log fragment, a name, a heading. Someone was looking for the same signal.

effects.text: The derelict held no survivors, but it held a story.

choices.label: Salvage what you can
choices.tone_note: Practical. The dead don't need engine parts.

effects.text: Stripped what was useful. Practical. Necessary.

choices.label: Log the position and leave it
choices.tone_note: A grave marker in coordinates.

effects.text: Noted the coordinates. Moved on.

text:
The station is alive with traffic. Cargo haulers queue for docking clearance. Merchants argue prices in three languages over open channels. A food vendor has set up a stall in the docking bay concourse, and the smell of something fried and heavily spiced cuts through the recycled air.

Your quartermaster is already compiling a shopping list. Your engineer is scanning the parts vendors. Someone on the crew — you're not sure who — has already left the ship.

For a few hours, the universe feels small and human and manageable. There are prices and schedules and people complaining about the docking fees. It's almost like being somewhere normal.

choices.label: Resupply and trade
choices.tone_note: The mundane business of staying alive.

effects.text: Resupplied. The ship feels a little more whole.

choices.label: Look for work on the job boards
choices.tone_note: Credits make the galaxy go around.

effects.description: Took a contract — delivery, investigation, or escort. Someone is counting on you now.

effects.text: Found work. The kind that keeps the tanks full.

choices.label: Let the crew have shore leave
choices.tone_note: They need it. You might too.

effects.text: The crew dispersed into the station. Came back lighter.

choices.label: Ask around for news and rumors
choices.tone_note: What's changed while you were in transit?

effects.description: Picked up rumors — faction movements, missing ships, a place that used to be safe and isn't anymore.

effects.text: Listened to the local talk. Filed it away.

text:
You find two of your crew in the observation lounge during the night watch. They've turned the lights down and are sitting in the glow of the starfield. One of them has a cup of something that stopped being hot a while ago. The other is just looking out.

They don't stop talking when you enter, which means either they trust you or they've stopped caring about rank. One of them is telling a story about a place they used to live. Details you haven't heard before — a street, a neighbor, the sound rain made on a particular roof.

The story doesn't have an ending. It just trails off into the hum of the ship.

choices.label: Sit down and listen
choices.tone_note: Being present. Not as the captain, just as someone who's here.

effects.text: Sat with the crew. Listened. Was present.

choices.label: Share something of your own
choices.tone_note: Vulnerability. Rare from the person who makes the decisions.

effects.description: Shared something personal with the crew. The walls are a little thinner now.

effects.text: You told them something true. It cost you nothing and everything.

choices.label: Leave them to it
choices.tone_note: Some moments aren't for you.

text:
The damage report is getting longer.

Your engineer reads it out in a flat voice that means things are bad enough to skip the sugar-coating. Hull breach in section four, sealed but not repaired. Port sensor array offline. Water recycler running at sixty percent. Life support is fine for now, which is the kind of reassurance that only matters because someone felt the need to say it.

"We can keep flying," she says. "I wouldn't recommend keeping flying."

The nearest station is on the charts. Whether it still exists is a different question. Whether it has the parts you need is another one after that.

choices.label: Head for the nearest station
choices.tone_note: Survival instinct. Get somewhere with walls that hold.

effects.text: Set course for the nearest repair facility.
Heading for repairs — hull integrity critical.

choices.label: Attempt field repairs with what we have
choices.tone_note: Making do. It's what you're good at.

effects.text: Jury-rigged repairs. Holding together, for now.

choices.label: Press on — we'll deal with it later
choices.tone_note: Stubbornness. Or maybe the destination matters more than the ship.

effects.text: Pressed on despite the damage. The crew noticed.

text:
In orbit around {system.name}, with nothing demanding your attention, you find yourself on the observation deck.

The star you're orbiting is unremarkable. A main sequence yellow, not unlike the one that warmed whatever world you came from. The light takes eight minutes to reach the inner planets and then keeps going forever, carrying information about this moment to places that won't receive it for millennia.

You're in that light now. A tiny perturbation in the data. If someone with a powerful enough telescope pointed it here in a thousand years, they might detect the faintest wobble — evidence that something was here. That something passed through.

Your navigator pings you about the next heading. The moment dissolves.

choices.label: Take a moment longer
choices.tone_note: The rare luxury of having nowhere urgent to be.

effects.text: Took a moment. The universe waited.

choices.label: Make a log entry about the view
choices.tone_note: Preserving something that doesn't need preserving.

effects.text: Added an entry to the log. Preserving what matters.

choices.label: Head to the bridge
choices.tone_note: Back to work. Stars don't pay the fuel bills.

text:
He finds you in the kind of bar that exists in every station — bad lighting, cheap drinks, nobody asking questions. He sits down without being invited and doesn't order anything.

"Your ship. It's fast enough and it's not flagged. I have cargo that needs to cross the border. Not weapons, not narcotics — I'm not stupid. Data cores. Research data. Proprietary stuff that belongs to people who'd rather it didn't leave their jurisdiction."

He slides a number across the table. It's a lot of money. Enough to keep you fueled for months.

"The people I'm taking this from won't miss it for weeks. The people I'm delivering it to will make sure it never gets traced back. Clean transaction. Everybody wins."

Nobody ever says 'everybody wins' when everybody actually wins.

choices.label: Hear him out fully before deciding
choices.tone_note: Information costs nothing. Commitment costs everything.

effects.description: The smuggler's operation is small but professional. The data cores contain proprietary research — genomic data, possibly valuable, definitely stolen.

effects.text: Heard him out. The details make it harder to say no, not easier.

choices.label: Accept the job
choices.tone_note: The money is real. The risk is abstract. For now.

effects.description: Carrying unknown cargo across a faction border. Someone is expecting delivery.

effects.text: Took the job. Didn't ask what's in the containers.

choices.label: Decline
choices.tone_note: Principles. Or maybe just caution.

effects.text: You declined. Life goes on.

choices.label: Walk away without a word
choices.tone_note: Not even worth a conversation.

text:
The colony is there on sensors — structures, power grid, atmospheric processors — but nobody answers the hail.

From orbit, the settlement looks intact. Buildings standing, roads visible, landing pad clear. But there are no lights. No heat signatures. No electromagnetic noise of a living community — no broadcasts, no chatter, no machines running their daily cycles.

The atmospheric processors are still working, slowly, on automatic. They'll keep going for years without maintenance. The air will be breathable for anyone who arrives. But nobody is arriving and nobody is breathing it.

The colony logs, if they still exist down there, might explain where everyone went. Or they might just stop, mid-entry, on an ordinary day.

choices.label: Land and investigate
choices.tone_note: Walking through someone else's abandoned life.

effects.description: Explored the abandoned colony. Logs stop mid-entry. No signs of violence, no signs of evacuation. People just... stopped being here.

effects.text: Walked through empty streets. The silence had weight.

choices.label: Scan for survivors from orbit
choices.tone_note: Hope tempered by distance.

effects.description: Orbital scan of the abandoned colony shows intact infrastructure, working atmosphere processors, and no biological signatures. Whatever happened, it wasn't structural failure.

effects.text: Scanned from orbit. The colony is intact. Just empty.

choices.label: Mark it on the charts and move on
choices.tone_note: You can't help the absent. But you can note they were here.

effects.text: Noted the coordinates. Moved on.

text:
It's not a distress call and it's not a beacon. Your comms officer can't classify it because it doesn't fit any classification.

The signal is mathematical — that much is clear. Repeating sequences with internal structure, prime number intervals, nested patterns that resolve and then complicate. It's coming from deep in the outer system, past the last planetary orbit, from a point in space where nothing should be.

"It's not random," your science officer says, unnecessarily. "And it's not any encoding I recognize."

The signal has been broadcasting for a while. Your sensors can estimate power output, and at this level, whatever is generating it has been running for a long time. Centuries, minimum.

Patient. Unhurried. Waiting for someone to listen.

choices.label: Follow the signal to its source
choices.tone_note: This is the kind of thing you crossed light-years to find.

effects.description: Investigated the signal source. Found something — wreckage, or a beacon, or something stranger. The data needs analysis.

effects.text: Followed the signal. What you found raises more questions than it answers.

choices.label: Record and analyze from here
choices.tone_note: Understanding before approaching.

effects.description: Signal analysis reveals nested mathematical structures — prime sequences, fractal patterns, and something that might be a coordinate system. It's not random. It's not any known encoding.

effects.text: Spent hours on the analysis. The signal is deliberate, ancient, and patient.

choices.label: Transmit a response
choices.tone_note: Bold. Possibly reckless. Undeniably human.

effects.description: Transmitted a response to the unknown signal. The signal paused for three seconds — the first interruption in centuries of repetition — then resumed with a new pattern.

effects.text: You spoke into the void. Something heard you.

choices.label: Note the coordinates and continue
choices.tone_note: Not everything that calls to you needs answering. Not today.

effects.text: Noted the coordinates. Moved on.

text:
The refueling station is exactly what it looks like: a few tanks, a docking arm, and someone who sells fuel. No bar, no trade board, no reason to stay longer than it takes to fill the tanks.

The attendant has the manner of a person who's been alone with machines for too long. She talks to the fuel pumps while she works. She tells your ship it's a good ship. She asks the docking clamps how they're feeling today.

When she notices you listening, she shrugs. "Company's company."

The fuel is reasonably priced. The solitude is free.

choices.label: Fuel up and chat for a bit
choices.tone_note: A small kindness in a large void.

effects.text: A small kindness at a quiet refueling stop.

choices.label: Just fuel, thanks
choices.tone_note: Transaction complete. Moving on.

choices.label: Ask if she's heard any news from the sector
choices.tone_note: People in quiet places hear things that busy people miss.

effects.description: Picked up rumors — faction movements, missing ships, a place that used to be safe and isn't anymore.

effects.text: Listened to the local talk. Filed it away.

text:
A ship drops out of the sensor noise about thirty thousand kilometers off your port side. Small, fast, no transponder. It's not approaching. It's matching your heading at a careful distance.

Your comms officer shakes her head — no hails, no identification, no response to your queries. Just a ship, sitting in your wake, watching.

Another contact appears on sensors. Same profile. Starboard side.

"They're not hiding," your security officer says. "They want us to know they're there."

A message arrives. Text only, no voice. Three words: DUMP YOUR CARGO.

choices.label: Comply — jettison cargo
choices.tone_note: Survival over pride. Cargo can be replaced.

effects.text: Cargo jettisoned. The pirates took it and vanished.

effects.description: Lost cargo to pirates in unclaimed space. The crew remembers.

choices.label: Ignore the message and hold course
choices.tone_note: Calling the bluff. Or forcing the hand.

effects.text: They fired a warning shot that wasn't entirely a warning. Hull took a hit, but they broke off.

effects.description: Faced down pirates and survived. They know your ship now.

choices.label: Open a channel — try to negotiate
choices.tone_note: Words before weapons.

effects.text: Negotiated a 'transit fee.' Everyone pretended it wasn't extortion.

choices.label: Run — full burn for the nearest system
choices.tone_note: Discretion. The better part of not dying.

effects.text: Burned hard and got clear. Cost fuel, saved everything else.

text:
The colony at {system.name} has grown.

Last time you were here — your time, months ago; their time, generations — it was a cluster of prefabs and determination. Now there's a city. Not a large city, not by any standard that matters, but a real one. Streets with names. A school. Arguments about zoning.

A delegation meets you at the landing pad. They're formal, a little nervous. You recognize the family resemblance in the lead official — the jaw, the way she stands. Her grandmother stood in this same spot when it was bare soil and hope.

There's a monument near the landing pad. A plaque with names on it. Founders. You recognize some of them.

The official follows your gaze. "We teach about the early days in school," she says. "The children think it's ancient history."

choices.label: Tell her about the founders you knew
choices.tone_note: Living memory offered to people who only have records.

effects.text: Told her stories about people she'd only read about. Her eyes were bright. Yours were not.

choices.label: Ask to see how the colony has changed
choices.tone_note: Seeing what time builds when you're not watching.

effects.text: They showed you the city. The school, the market, the park they named after someone you once knew.

choices.label: Conduct your business and move on
choices.tone_note: You've learned not to linger in places that moved on without you.

effects.text: Resupplied. The ship feels a little more whole.

text:
The trade board at the station is chaos. Prices are shifting in real time — fuel up twelve percent, then down eight, then up again. A Corridor Guild representative stands near the exchange terminal, watching numbers move with the calm of someone who already knows how this ends.

Your navigator leans over. "They're squeezing out the independents. Anyone who bought at yesterday's rate is underwater."

The Guild rep catches you watching and raises a hand — not a wave, exactly. An acknowledgment. The kind that comes with an invitation attached.

"Captain. If you're looking to move cargo, we can offer preferred rates. Our logistics network is... comprehensive."

choices.label: Accept the Guild's rates — business is business
choices.tone_note: Pragmatic. The galaxy runs on deals like this.

effects.description: Accepted Corridor Guild preferred rates. They'll remember — and they'll expect reciprocity.

effects.text: Signed on with the Guild's network. Good prices. Strings attached.

choices.label: Buy fuel at the inflated independent rate instead
choices.tone_note: Principle has a price. You can afford it today.

effects.text: Paid more than you had to. The independent seller looked grateful.

choices.label: Ask what 'comprehensive' means, exactly
choices.tone_note: Curiosity. Every network has edges worth knowing.

effects.description: The Corridor Guild's logistics network extends further than official records suggest. They know shipping routes, cargo manifests, and who's moving what — including near the frontier.

effects.text: Learned more than the Guild rep intended to share.

text:
It's in the docking bay, tucked behind a maintenance panel that shouldn't be loose but is. A data chip, commercial grade, nothing remarkable. Except someone scratched a symbol into the casing — a lattice pattern, geometric, precise.

Your comms officer turns it over. "This is a dead drop. Someone left it for someone. Maybe us, maybe not."

The chip is unencrypted. That's either trust or a trap. The data inside is a set of coordinates, a name you don't recognize, and a single line of text: THEY KNOW ABOUT THE SIGNAL.

The scratched symbol catches the light as you turn the chip. Someone in the shadows is paying attention to the same mystery you are.

choices.label: Copy the data and leave the chip where you found it
choices.tone_note: Take the knowledge, leave no trace.

effects.description: Copied data from a Lattice dead drop. Coordinates, a name, and a warning: someone else knows about the signal.

effects.text: Took the knowledge. Left no trace.

choices.label: Take the chip — if it was meant for you, you'll need it
choices.tone_note: If they wanted to reach you, now they know they did.

effects.description: Took a Lattice dead drop chip. The coordinates and name might be useful. Taking the chip means they know you found it.

effects.text: Pocketed the chip. Someone will notice it's gone.

choices.label: Destroy it — you don't need shadow network complications
choices.tone_note: Some doors you don't open.

effects.text: Crushed the chip under your boot. Some connections aren't worth making.

text:
They've built something here, in the distorted space where time moves differently. Not a station — a vigil. A ring of small ships linked by tether cables, drifting in formation around nothing in particular. Or nothing your sensors can identify.

The Order of the Quiet Star. You've heard of them. Pilgrims who believe that time distortion is a wound in the universe, and that if you listen at the edges of that wound, you can hear what tore it.

A voice comes through on an open channel. Unhurried. The kind of voice that has been saying the same words for a very long time.

"Traveler. You feel it too, don't you? The way time bends here. We have been listening for eleven years, ship-time. Outside, forty-three years have passed. We have heard things."

A pause.

"Would you like to know what we've heard?"

choices.label: Yes. Tell me everything.
choices.tone_note: Awe and hunger. This is what the edges of the map are for.

effects.description: The Order describes patterns in the time distortion — rhythmic fluctuations that correlate with the signal's frequency. They believe the distortion and the signal share a source.

effects.text: Listened. What they described changes the shape of everything.

choices.label: What do you know about the signal?
choices.tone_note: Direct. The mission pulls at you even here.

effects.description: The Order has been tracking the signal independently. Their data suggests the source is deep in distorted space — somewhere time runs so slowly it might as well have stopped.

effects.text: They know about the signal. They've been listening longer than you have.

choices.label: Join the vigil for a time — listen for yourself
choices.tone_note: Patience. Some things you have to hear firsthand.

effects.description: Spent time in the vigil, listening at the edge of distorted space. Something was there — not a sound exactly, but a structure in the silence.

effects.text: Sat in the quiet. Heard something. Can't explain what.

choices.label: Thank them and move on
choices.tone_note: Respect without commitment. Not everyone who listens hears.

text:
The workshop is built into the side of an asteroid, accessible through a docking tunnel that doesn't appear on any chart. Your navigator found it the old way — following transponder echoes that someone left for people who know to look.

Inside, it's surprisingly clean. Three bays, two occupied. Tools organized with the care of someone who respects their work. The woman running the place has hands stained with engine coolant and eyes that don't miss much.

"Ashfall Salvage," she says, like you should know the name. "We fix things out here where nobody else will. Pay is flexible. Questions are not included."

She glances at your hull readout on her display. "Your ship has seen better days. I can help with that. Standard rate, or if you're carrying anything interesting, we can talk trade."

choices.label: Pay for repairs — credits
choices.tone_note: Clean transaction. Worth the price for hull integrity.

effects.text: Professional work. No questions asked. Hull integrity restored.

choices.label: Offer to trade cargo for repair work
choices.tone_note: Barter economy. Out here, flexibility matters.

effects.text: Traded cargo for repairs. Fair deal, out where fair is relative.

choices.label: Ask what else they can do besides repairs
choices.tone_note: Curiosity about what lives at the edges.

effects.description: Ashfall Salvage runs more than a repair shop — they move people, cargo, and information across borders the official routes don't cross. The frontier has its own economy.

effects.text: Asked around. The frontier has layers you hadn't seen.

choices.label: Too risky — leave
choices.tone_note: Paranoia is a survival trait.

text:
A {civ.name} cruiser drops in three kilometers off your bow, close enough to read the hull markings with your eyes. Hegemony Military Command. Not patrol boats this time — a proper warship, running a sweep.

Your comms light up before you can react. "Independent vessel, you are in a security zone. Cut engines and prepare for inspection. This is not a request."

The officer who boards is thorough, professional, and takes her time. She scans cargo, checks crew manifests against a database you can't see, and lingers over your navigation logs.

"You've been spending time near the frontier," she notes. Not a question. "Lot of traffic headed that way lately. Most of it doesn't come back the same."

She pauses on something in your logs. You can't see what.

"You're clear. For now. But Captain — be careful what you go looking for out there."

choices.label: Ask what she means about traffic not coming back the same
choices.tone_note: The warning is the interesting part.

effects.description: The military officer let slip that ships returning from deep-frontier distorted space show anomalous navigation log corruption. Hegemony Command is tracking it.

effects.text: She said more than she meant to. Or exactly as much as she intended.

choices.label: Comply silently — don't invite further scrutiny
choices.tone_note: Endure the process. Say nothing memorable.

effects.text: Inspection passed without incident.

choices.label: Ask about restricted zones near your planned route
choices.tone_note: Practical. Use the encounter to plan ahead.

effects.description: Learned that Hegemony Military Command has designated certain frontier routes as restricted — specifically near systems with high time distortion. They're not saying why.

effects.text: Restricted zones. Near the frontier. Near the signal. That's not a coincidence.

text:
The Spacers' hall is easy to find — it's wherever the dock workers eat. Here, it's a converted cargo module with benches, a food printer that's seen better decades, and a board covered in handwritten notes.

Crew postings. Route warnings. Someone selling a sensor array, used, mostly functional. A drawing of something that might be a star system or might be a joke. The usual noise of people who live between the stars.

An older spacer with a Collective badge sewn onto a jacket that's older than your ship waves you over. "You're the one with the Persistence, right? Been hearing your transponder code at some interesting ports."

He slides a drink across the table. "Sit. I've got a few things you might want to hear, and a favor I might want to ask."

choices.label: Sit and listen — spacers always know something useful
choices.tone_note: The network matters. These people are the galaxy's memory.

effects.description: The spacer's network has noticed unusual shipping pattern changes — routes being rerouted around certain frontier systems, contracts drying up near distorted space.

effects.text: Bought a round. Heard three rumors. Two of them might even be true.

choices.label: Ask about the favor first
choices.tone_note: Practical. Know the cost before you accept the gift.

effects.description: The spacer needs a message delivered to a contact at a frontier system. Simple job, he says. Spacers don't ask for favors unless the official channels won't work.

effects.text: Took the job. A message, a destination, and a handshake.

choices.label: Check the job board instead — you'll find your own leads
choices.tone_note: Self-reliance. You don't owe anyone conversation.

effects.description: Took a contract — delivery, investigation, or escort. Someone is counting on you now.

effects.text: Found work. The kind that keeps the tanks full.

text:
The message arrives through three relays, stripped of metadata, timed to land when your comms officer is on a meal break. Professional.

"Captain. We trade in information. You have been asking questions that align with questions we have been paid to answer. There may be mutual benefit."

No name. No affiliation. But the routing pattern has a signature you've seen before — the same lattice geometry, the same careful anonymity.

"We can provide intelligence on faction movements, restricted zones, and certain... signals of interest. Our rates are reasonable. Our discretion is absolute. Reply on this channel if interested."

The channel will close in sixty seconds. The timer is already running.

choices.label: Reply — you need every advantage you can get
choices.tone_note: The shadows have something you need. The cost is being known by them.

effects.description: Established a channel with a Lattice intelligence broker. They have information about the signal and faction movements near distorted space. The relationship is transactional — for now.

effects.text: Made contact with the shadows. They had exactly what you needed.

choices.label: Reply but negotiate terms first
choices.tone_note: Interest, but on your terms. Show them you're not desperate.

effects.description: Negotiated limited terms with a Lattice broker. They provided partial intelligence — enough to be useful, not enough to feel comfortable.

effects.text: Bargained them down. They respected the counter-offer.

choices.label: Let the timer expire
choices.tone_note: Silence is an answer too. They'll understand.

text:
The pilgrim finds you in the observation bay, where the stars look wrong. Time moves strangely here — you can feel it in the way light shifts, the way sounds arrive a half-beat late, the way your own heartbeat seems to be keeping time with something else.

"You are looking for the source," the pilgrim says. Not asks. Says.

She's old in a way that's hard to place. Ship-time old or real-time old — in space like this, the distinction loses meaning.

"We have spent decades listening at the edges. The signal you chase — we have heard its echoes here. Not the signal itself. Its shadow. The shape of what sent it."

She draws something on the fogged viewport. A pattern. Mathematical. Familiar in a way you can't quite place.

"It is not a message. It is a question. And it has been waiting a very long time for someone to understand what it's asking."

choices.label: What is the question?
choices.tone_note: The mission tightens. This is real. This matters.

effects.description: The pilgrim described the signal as a question — mathematical, patient, waiting for comprehension. The pattern she drew matches nothing in human mathematics, but your science officer thinks it resembles a topology proof.

effects.text: The pattern burns in your mind. It means something. You almost understand.

choices.label: Record the pattern she drew — analyze it later
choices.tone_note: Collect the evidence. Trust the science over the mysticism.

effects.description: Recorded the pattern the pilgrim drew on the viewport. Analysis pending, but initial comparison suggests non-human mathematical notation.

effects.text: Captured the image. Science will find what faith can't.

choices.label: How did you know about our mission?
choices.tone_note: Suspicion and wonder in equal measure.

effects.description: The pilgrim knew about the mission — claims the Order has contacts who share information about signal-seekers. Unclear whether this means allies or surveillance.

effects.text: She smiled. 'The distortion tells us who is listening.' Make of that what you will.

text:
The station's trade deck is a low-ceilinged hall that smells of lubricant and recycled air. Docking fees have already been deducted from your account. A broker materializes at your elbow before you've finished sealing the airlock — they can smell fresh cargo the way predators smell blood.

Prices here are standard, maybe a fraction better if your reputation precedes you. The broker quotes figures with practiced indifference, but their eyes keep flicking to your cargo manifest.

choices.label: Trade at standard rates
choices.tone_note: Routine, efficient. The mundane machinery of survival.

choices.label: Push for better terms
choices.tone_note: A negotiation that leaves both sides slightly unsatisfied — the best kind.

choices.label: Browse and leave without buying
choices.tone_note: Sometimes you just need to see what's available.

text:
Your comms light up with a hail on a frequency that isn't in any official registry. A small vessel, barely more than an engine strapped to a cargo pod, drifts at the edge of your sensor range. The pilot's voice is scratchy, unhurried.

"Got some things. Might have what you need. Might not. No records, no receipts, no questions." A pause. "Also no refunds."

choices.label: Trade carefully — inspect everything
choices.tone_note: Cautious but fair. Two ships passing in the dark.

choices.label: Buy everything they have at a premium
choices.tone_note: Desperation or opportunity — hard to tell the difference out here.

choices.label: Decline and move on
choices.tone_note: Trust is expensive. You can't afford it today.

text:
Your sensors flag something that shouldn't be here — a pocket of spacetime where the readings fold back on themselves like a page corner. Not dangerous, not yet, but deeply wrong in a way that makes your navigator go quiet.

Closer scans reveal structure within the anomaly. Patterns that look almost deliberate. Your science officer wants to deploy probes. Your engineer wants to leave. Both make good arguments.

choices.label: Deploy probes and analyze at safe distance
choices.tone_note: Careful, methodical wonder. The awe of the careful mind.

effects.description: Anomaly readings suggest deliberate structure — someone or something built this

effects.text: The data streams back slowly, each packet stranger than the last.

choices.label: Move closer for direct sensor readings
choices.tone_note: Reckless curiosity. The readings are extraordinary. The cost might be too.

effects.description: Direct exposure to the anomaly revealed something your instruments can barely describe

choices.label: Log the coordinates and move on
choices.tone_note: Restraint. Not every door needs to be opened.

effects.text: You mark the anomaly on your charts. Some mysteries keep.

text:
The structure orbits the fourth planet like a second moon, except moons don't have right angles. It's old — your instruments can tell you that much, though they disagree on how old. Centuries? Millennia? The margin of error is embarrassing.

Atmosphere scans show a breathable pocket inside, maintained by systems that still work despite everything. Someone built this to last. Whether they lasted too is the question your crew is carefully not asking.

choices.label: Send an away team to explore
choices.tone_note: The weight of deep time. Walking through someone else's abandoned home.

effects.description: The ruin contained writing in a language your systems can almost parse

effects.text: The team returns hours later, quiet and carrying samples.

choices.label: Scan remotely — don't risk the crew
choices.tone_note: Prudent. But some things can only be understood up close.

effects.text: Remote scans reveal architectural details but miss whatever the builders left inside.

choices.label: Salvage what you can from the exterior
choices.tone_note: Practical. The dead don't need their possessions. Probably.

text:
The repair bay is cramped and smells of ozone and welding flux. A technician with oil-dark hands looks over your damage report with the expression of someone who's seen worse but isn't sure they want to say so.

"Can fix it. Most of it. Gonna cost you, though — parts aren't cheap out here, and neither am I." They tap your hull assessment. "Your engineer could handle the small stuff. I'll do the structural work."

choices.label: Full repair — fix everything
choices.tone_note: Expensive but thorough. The ship deserves it.

choices.label: Hull only — patch what's critical
choices.tone_note: Triage. Spend where it matters most.

choices.label: Just the diagnostics — your engineer will handle the rest
choices.tone_note: Frugal. Your crew can do the work; you just needed the blueprint.

effects.text: The technician's diagnostic reveals problems your own scans missed. Knowledge is worth something.

text:
Your sensor officer runs a deep sweep of the system — the kind that takes hours, not minutes. The bridge goes quiet as data accumulates, each pass revealing another layer of what this corner of space contains.

Most of it is ordinary. Rocks and radiation and the ancient light of distant stars. But ordinary is relative, and your instruments are patient.

choices.label: Focus on astronomical data — star composition, orbital mechanics
choices.tone_note: The quiet satisfaction of knowing where you are in the universe.

effects.text: The scan reveals detailed stellar data. Your navigator updates the charts with new precision.

choices.label: Scan for signals — artificial emissions, distress calls, anything deliberate
choices.tone_note: Listening for voices in the dark. Sometimes the dark answers.

effects.description: Long-range signal scan detected a repeating pattern from the outer system

effects.text: The scan picks up something. Faint, regular, and definitely not natural.

choices.label: Quick sweep — don't waste the fuel
choices.tone_note: Impatient, practical. There's somewhere else to be.

effects.text: A cursory scan. Nothing obvious. But obvious things are rarely what matter.

text:
Sensors pick up a hull drifting in the outer reaches of {system.name}. No power signature. No beacon. The design is unfamiliar — not Hegemony, not colonial. Something older.

{crew.random.name} runs a structural scan. "Intact enough to board. Atmosphere's gone, but the hull geometry suggests cargo bays amidships. Could be worth a look."

The {ship.name} pulls alongside. Through the viewport, the derelict's surface is pitted but unbreached. Whatever killed this ship, it wasn't weapons fire.

choices.label: Suit up and board

effects.text: Crossed the gap. The airlock opened easier than it should have.

choices.tone_note: Commitment. Whatever's in there, you'll see it firsthand.

choices.label: Send a remote probe instead

effects.description: Probe footage from an unidentified derelict near {system.name}. The interior was dark. The probe lost signal after four minutes.

effects.text: The probe's feed cut out deep inside the ship. The last frame showed an open door that shouldn't have been open.

choices.tone_note: Cautious. You'll know less, but you'll be safe.

choices.label: Mark the coordinates and move on

effects.description: Unidentified derelict catalogued near {system.name}. Never boarded.

effects.text: Filed the location. Someone else's mystery.

choices.tone_note: Pragmatic. Not every door needs to be opened.

text:
The interior is wrong in ways that take a moment to register.

The corridors are the right shape for a ship — functional, braced, designed for people to move through. But the proportions are off. Ceilings too high. Doorways too wide. The handholds along the walls are spaced for someone with longer arms.

Deeper in, you find what might be a bridge. The consoles are dark but intact. There's writing on the bulkheads — dense, layered script in a language your translator doesn't recognize. Not because it's corrupted. Because it was never in the database.

{crew.random.name} finds something on what might be the captain's station. A small object, dense and warm to the touch even through suit gloves. It pulses faintly when held.

choices.label: Take the object back to the ship

effects.description: Recovered a pulsing artifact from an alien derelict. It responds to proximity. It might be responding to us.

effects.text: Brought it aboard. It's in the cargo bay now, pulsing steadily. No one sleeps well that first night.

choices.tone_note: Acquisitive. Curious. Maybe unwise.

choices.label: Document everything but leave it

effects.description: Thorough scans of an alien derelict's interior. Script samples. Architectural measurements. Left the artifact in place.

effects.text: Recorded everything. Touched nothing. The professional choice, and the crew respects it.

choices.tone_note: Disciplined. The data matters more than the trophy.

choices.label: Try to activate the consoles

effects.description: Attempted to activate alien bridge consoles. Something responded. Brief burst of power through the derelict — lights, sounds in frequencies that hurt. Then silence. The ship's orientation shifted three degrees toward the galactic core.

effects.text: For two seconds, the ship was alive. Whatever it showed you in that moment, you can't quite remember. But you know it was pointing somewhere.

choices.tone_note: Bold. Reckless. The kind of thing that changes trajectories.

text:
As the {ship.name} drops out of FTL at {system.name}, {crew.random.name} looks up from the comms console with an expression you can't read.

"That signal. The one we recorded. It's here too."

Not the same signal — the same structure. The same mathematical backbone, the same nested prime sequences. But the content is different. As if the signal at the last system was a question, and this one is part of the answer.

It's been waiting here. It might have been waiting everywhere.

choices.label: Cross-reference the two signals

effects.description: Two instances of the same signal structure at different systems. Cross-referencing reveals a spatial pattern — the signals form a bearing. Something is triangulating.

effects.text: The math resolves into a direction. Not a location yet. But a direction.

choices.tone_note: Analytical. The pieces are connecting.

choices.label: Record it and stay focused on the mission

effects.description: Second instance of the unknown signal recorded at {system.name}. Pattern is spreading. Not investigating further.

effects.text: Added to the file. The file is getting thick.

choices.tone_note: Disciplined. But it's getting harder to ignore.

text:
Your sensors pick up a metallic signature among the asteroids — too regular to be natural. As you close in, the shape resolves: a cargo pod, still sealed, tumbling slowly between two house-sized rocks.

The markings have been scrubbed, but the pod is intact. Someone went to a lot of trouble to hide this here. Or lost it.

choices.label: Retrieve the pod

effects.description: Found a hidden cargo pod in the asteroid belt. No markings, no transponder. Someone hid this deliberately.

effects.text: Your crew hauls the pod aboard. It's heavier than it looks.

choices.tone_note: Curiosity and caution in equal measure.

choices.label: Scan it from a distance and log the coordinates

effects.description: Located a hidden cargo pod in the belt. Coordinates logged but cargo left undisturbed.

effects.text: Safer this way. You note the coordinates and move on.

choices.tone_note: Prudent. Whatever's in there isn't worth the risk.

choices.label: Leave it — could be bait

effects.text: Could be salvage. Could be a trap. You don't need to find out which.

choices.tone_note: Hard-won wisdom.

text:
The station bar is the kind of place where information has a price and silence is free. You find a corner table and order something that won't kill you.

Conversation washes over you in fragments. Most of it is worthless — dock schedules, complaints about fuel prices, someone's ex. But you catch a thread.

choices.label: Listen carefully to the traders

effects.description: Overheard traders discussing unusual shipping routes — cargo being diverted to systems with no registered population.

effects.text: You file it away. Could be smuggling. Could be something else.

choices.tone_note: Patient intelligence gathering.

choices.label: Buy a round and ask around

effects.description: A freighter pilot mentioned signals at unusual frequencies near the outer system. Said his instruments couldn't identify the source.

effects.text: Credits well spent. People talk when the drinks flow.

choices.tone_note: Social investment. Building the picture.

choices.label: Finish your drink and leave

effects.text: Sometimes a drink is just a drink.

choices.tone_note: Not every moment needs to be significant.

text:
The surface survey picks up geometric patterns beneath the topsoil — too precise for geology, too old for any known civilization. Your crew member calls you over:

"Captain, you'll want to see this."

Excavation reveals carved stone, not native to this world. The symbols don't match anything in your database. They've been here for a very long time.

choices.label: Document everything and take samples

effects.description: Found ancient alien ruins on a planetary surface. Carved symbols in non-native stone. Predates all known civilizations.

effects.text: Hours of careful work. Your science officer is practically vibrating with excitement.

choices.tone_note: The wonder of discovery. Arrival-like reverence.

choices.label: Quick scan and move on — we're not archaeologists

effects.description: Detected ancient ruins on the surface. Basic scan logged but no detailed investigation conducted.

effects.text: The sensors capture what they can. Somewhere, a researcher would weep at your haste.

choices.tone_note: Pragmatic. You have a mission.

choices.label: Mark the coordinates and report to the nearest faction

effects.description: Reported alien ruins to local faction authorities. Coordinates shared. They seemed very interested.

effects.text: You file the report. The response comes back almost too quickly. They were already looking for something like this.

choices.tone_note: Responsible. But who benefits from this knowledge?

text:
The anomaly doesn't look like anything. That's the problem.

Your sensors insist something is here — mass, energy, a gravitational signature that bends light just enough to notice. But the viewport shows nothing. Empty space, stars behind it, everything normal.

Except the stars behind it are wrong. The constellations don't match your charts. As if whatever is here is showing you a different sky.

{crew.random.name} says, very quietly: "Captain, our chronometer just lost six minutes."

choices.label: Hold position. Record everything.

effects.description: Encountered a spatial anomaly in deep space — invisible to visual sensors but present on instruments. Chronometer lost six minutes during observation. Stars visible through the anomaly don't match known charts.

effects.text: The recording equipment runs. Your crew watches the nothing that shouldn't be there. Nobody speaks for a very long time.

choices.tone_note: Solaris. The unknowable, observed.

choices.label: Move closer. Carefully.

effects.description: Approached the deep space anomaly. At closer range, instruments detected a pattern in the gravitational signature — mathematical, recursive, intentional.

effects.text: The closer you get, the more your instruments disagree with each other. But something in the data looks almost like a pattern. Almost like a message.

choices.tone_note: Courage or recklessness. The mystery deepens.

choices.label: Full reverse. Now.

effects.description: Detected and retreated from a deep space anomaly. Brief contact. Something is out here.

effects.text: The engines fire. The anomaly recedes. The lost six minutes never come back.

choices.tone_note: Discretion. Some things you're not ready for.
